tweets.text:
Facts vs Feelings Ep. 193🎙️

@RyanDetrick & @sonusvarghese on Greenspan's passing, Warsh's debut, and a Fed day that rattled markets:

🪦 What Greenspan's 18 years actually delivered: +290% on the S&P, two burst bubbles, and the worst decade equity investors have ever had

📋 Warsh's first statement ran ~130 words — the shortest non-emergency Fed statement in modern history. And no dot plot.

⚖️ The dot plot went 9-9 on a 2026 hike (three months ago, 12 of 19 saw a cut)

📈 The 2-yr yield jumped 16 bps on Fed day — the biggest single-day move on a Fed decision since 2008

🤔 The contrarian read: real policy rates are MORE accommodative now than they were in March

📱 Apple's raising iPhone prices as chip PPI runs north of 100% annualized

🏆 Dow, Russell 2000, and S&P MidCap 400 all closed at record highs and more!

All links below 👇 🎙️ 🔥

tweets.text:
BREAKING: The Fed’s balance sheet rose +$11 billion in the week ending June 17th, to $6.74 trillion, the highest since March 2025.

Total assets have risen +$162.8 billion since the start of the year.

This has been driven by Treasury holdings, which have surged +$251.8 billion over this period, to $4.49 trillion, the highest since June 2024.

At the same time, holdings of Mortgage-Backed Securities have declined -$74.2 billion, to $1.96 trillion, the lowest since September 2020.

The Fed's balance sheet is now 76% above pre-pandemic levels.

The Fed's balance sheet continues to expand.
tweets.full_text:
BREAKING: The Fed’s balance sheet rose +$11 billion in the week ending June 17th, to $6.74 trillion, the highest since March 2025.

Total assets have risen +$162.8 billion since the start of the year.

This has been driven by Treasury holdings, which have surged +$251.8 billion over this period, to $4.49 trillion, the highest since June 2024.

At the same time, holdings of Mortgage-Backed Securities have declined -$74.2 billion, to $1.96 trillion, the lowest since September 2020.

The Fed's balance sheet is now 76% above pre-pandemic levels.

The Fed's balance sheet continues to expand.

tweets.text:
The US wealth divide has never been bigger:

The top 20% of earners now account for ~58% of all personal spending in the US, the highest proportion on record. 

At the same time, the bottom 80% account for just ~42%, the lowest on record.

This comes as personal outlays growth for the top 20% has averaged +8.3% per year since the 2020 pandemic, almost double the +4.5% seen for the bottom 80%.

Over the last year, the gap has widened further, with the top 20% seeing a +6.5% increase in spending, compared to +2.6% for the bottom 80%.

To put this into perspective, in the 1990s, both groups accounted for roughly equal proportions of total personal spending, at ~50% each.

Asset owners are the only winners in this economy.
tweets.full_text:
The US wealth divide has never been bigger:

At the same time, the bottom 80% account for just ~42%, the lowest on record.

This comes as personal outlays growth for the top 20% has averaged +8.3% per year since the 2020 pandemic, almost double the +4.5% seen for the bottom 80%.

Over the last year, the gap has widened further, with the top 20% seeing a +6.5% increase in spending, compared to +2.6% for the bottom 80%.

To put this into perspective, in the 1990s, both groups accounted for roughly equal proportions of total personal spending, at ~50% each.

Asset owners are the only winners in this economy.

tweets.text:
Everyone is piling into US stocks.

Foreign holdings of US equities jumped +$2.0 trillion in April, to a record $23.2 trillion.

This figure has more than DOUBLED since the 2022 bear market.

By comparison, during the 2020 pandemic, non-US investors held $7.5 trillion of US equities, or 68% less.

Foreign investors now own ~30.5% of the $76 trillion US equity market.

Furthermore, foreign investors’ allocation to US equities as a percentage of US financial assets is up to 63%, an all-time high and 10 percentage points above the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble peak.

Foreign investors are all-in on US stocks.

tweets.text:
Mirendil Co-Founder and CEO Behnam Neyshabur explains the path to self-accelerating AI:

" When you think about what does a scientist or an engineer do, the main skill they have is get very deep in a domain... and build a very sharp expertise that accumulates over time."

"As you kind of become expert in a domain, your expertise is becoming sharper and sharper in something that has a tiny bit of volume in the whole set of all possible things you could be working on."

"And getting to that point, being able to get there is a capability that is needed to advance all areas of science."

"What that view means is that you have to work on what does it take to direct a system toward like a particular angle and make fast improvement, and that's the self-accelerating AI technology."

@bneyshabur @MirendilAI
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Today, I’m excited to formally announce @MirendilAI with my amazing co-founders Harsh Mehta, Shayan Salehian, and Tara Rezaei!

We’re fortunate to work with @a16z and @kleinerperkins, who led our seed round of $200M, followed by a major investment from NVIDIA, among others. https://t.co/94ZVqEX2Rh
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
We're thrilled to lead Mirendil's $200M seed round.

Frontier AI work has been locked inside a few big labs.

@MirendilAI is building a system that can help anyone do AI work: they train frontier models that are expert at AI R&D and build the product around it. The result is a system that loops over research and engineering problems on its own, making progress without human intervention.

It's like a coding agent built for AI research that controls its own GPUs.

Mirendil is one of the few teams with the experience and the priors to make the end-to-end system work. @bneyshabur, @HarshMeh1a, @shayan_, and @tararezaeikh come out of Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and xAI.

Welcome to the a16z family, Mirendil.

By @BornsteinMatt and @MaikaThoughts
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Today, I’m excited to formally announce @MirendilAI with my amazing co-founders Harsh Mehta, Shayan Salehian, and Tara Rezaei!

We’re fortunate to work with @a16z and @kleinerperkins, who led our seed round of $200M, followed by a major investment from NVIDIA, among others. https://t.co/94ZVqEX2Rh

It's official:

Crypto has now erased more than HALF of its value in just 8 months.

On October 6th, 2025, the total market cap of crypto hit a record $4.3 trillion.

Crypto is in desperate need of a new narrative.

tweets.text:
I understand why you want to discuss my incentives instead of your predictions so far about AI vs. the reality on the ground.
 
$NODE is an active ETF. We can own exchanges, brokers, stablecoin infrastructure, tokenization, payments, miners, digital assets directly (via ETFs) and more. I don't have to own these names at these weights.

We own AI and power because we developed conviction early that AI is real, the U.S. underinvested in electricity for two decades, and intelligence will not be fully globalized. Those forces are creating multiple demand shocks for power and powered land, lowering the cost of capital and dramstically improving earnings quality for many companies we own. 

And yes, I'm hedged. Despite being a top ~10 $NODE shareholder personally and not buying any other security this year, most of my net worth remains in broad U.S. index funds.

Ossified.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: @matthew_sigel Lol, you literally run an AI fund, Matthew. You are, by definition, a permanent glass-half-full guy in this sector. I have a lot of AI/DC-related shorts, but no more than their representation in the indices. My “clock” is hedged, but can you say the same?

“It can’t go down much more.”

Strategy ($MSTR) is now down over 80% from its high.

tweets.text:
Gasoline futures have a seasonal problem.

Since 2007, long positions from trading day 124 through 238 gained in 3 years and lost in 18. 
The win rate was 14%.

Moves of more than $10K skewed negative by 12 to 2.

Read full analysis: https://t.co/orFF5tZ9qj https://t.co/clAo1Ufuh9
tweets.full_text:
Gasoline futures have a seasonal problem.

Since 2007, long positions from trading day 124 through 238 gained in 3 years and lost in 18. 
The win rate was 14%.

Moves of more than $10K skewed negative by 12 to 2.

tweets.text:
Stock prices do lead the economy, just not the part you think. 

Stocks turn a couple of months ahead of the "Aggregate Economy" but lag the "Cyclical Economy", the part that actually drives recessions.

(Also, stocks are a better predictor of upturns vs. downturns)
